commands.ls: List the contents of a directory. Shows files and folders.
commands.cd: Change the current working directory. Move between folders.
commands.pwd: Print working directory. Shows where you are in the filesystem.
commands.mkdir: Make directory. Creates a new folder.
commands.rmdir: Remove directory. Deletes an empty folder.
commands.rm: Remove files or directories. Permanently deletes — no Trash.
commands.cp: Copy files or directories from one location to another.
commands.mv: Move or rename files and directories.
commands.touch: Create an empty file, or update a file's last-modified timestamp.
commands.cat: Print the contents of a file to the screen.
commands.less: View a file one screen at a time. Press q to quit.
commands.more: View a file one screen at a time (older alternative to less).
commands.head: Show the first lines of a file (default: 10 lines).
commands.tail: Show the last lines of a file. Used to follow log files with -f.
commands.grep: Search files for lines matching a pattern.
commands.find: Search for files and directories by name, type, or other criteria.
commands.echo: Print text to the screen or write it to a file.
commands.printf: Print formatted text. More control than echo.
commands.read: Read a line of input from the user into a variable.
commands.sort: Sort lines of text alphabetically or numerically.
commands.uniq: Remove duplicate adjacent lines from sorted input.
commands.wc: Word count. Count lines, words, and characters in a file.
commands.cut: Extract columns or fields from each line of a file.
commands.awk: Pattern-scanning and text-processing language. Good for columns.
commands.sed: Stream editor. Find and replace text in a file or stream.
commands.tr: Translate or delete characters in a stream of text.
commands.diff: Compare two files line by line and show differences.
commands.patch: Apply a diff file to update a source file.
commands.xargs: Build commands from standard input. Passes output as arguments.
commands.tee: Read from standard input, write to a file AND the screen.
commands.pipe: The | character. Sends output of one command as input to another.
commands.chmod: Change file permissions (read, write, execute) for owner/group/others.
commands.chown: Change the owner and/or group of a file.
commands.chgrp: Change the group owner of a file.
commands.stat: Display detailed file or filesystem metadata.
commands.file: Identify what type of file something is (text, binary, image, etc.).
commands.ln: Create a link (shortcut) to another file. -s for symbolic link.
commands.readlink: Print the target of a symbolic link.
commands.realpath: Print the absolute path, resolving all symlinks.
commands.basename: Strip the directory portion of a file path.
commands.dirname: Return the directory portion of a file path.
commands.du: Disk usage. Show how much space files and directories use.
commands.df: Disk free. Show available and used space on mounted filesystems.
commands.mount: Attach a filesystem to the directory tree.
commands.top: Interactive process viewer. Shows CPU and memory usage live.
commands.htop: Enhanced interactive process viewer (must be installed).
commands.kill: Send a signal to a process, usually to stop it.
commands.killall: Kill all processes with a given name.
commands.pkill: Kill processes by name or other attributes.
commands.pgrep: Find process IDs matching a name or pattern.
commands.jobs: List background jobs running in the current shell.
commands.bg: Resume a stopped job in the background.
commands.fg: Bring a background job to the foreground.
commands.nohup: Run a command that keeps running after you log out.
commands.nice: Start a process with a modified scheduling priority.
commands.renice: Change the scheduling priority of a running process.
commands.sleep: Pause execution for a specified number of seconds.
commands.wait: Wait for a background process to finish.
commands.time: Measure how long a command takes to run.
commands.env: Print all environment variables, or run a command in a modified environment.
commands.export: Make a shell variable available to child processes.
commands.set: Set shell options or display all shell variables.
commands.unset: Remove a variable or function from the shell environment.
commands.source: Execute a file's commands in the current shell (same as dot command).
commands.alias: Create a shortcut name for a longer command.
commands.history: Show a list of previously typed commands.
commands.which: Show the full path of a command that would be executed.
commands.whereis: Find the binary, source, and manual page for a command.
commands.type: Describe how a name would be interpreted by the shell.
commands.man: Manual pages. Shows the full documentation for a command.
commands.info: Show documentation in info format (alternative to man).
commands.help: Show brief help for a shell built-in command.
commands.reset: Reset the terminal to a clean state.
commands.logout: Log out of a login shell.
commands.sudo: Run a command as the superuser (root) or another user.
commands.su: Switch user. Start a shell as another user.
commands.groups: List the groups the current user belongs to.
commands.ssh: Securely connect to a remote machine over the network.
commands.scp: Securely copy files between machines over SSH.
commands.sftp: Securely transfer files interactively over SSH.
commands.rsync: Efficiently sync files between directories or machines.
commands.curl: Transfer data to or from a server using URLs.
commands.wget: Download files from the web non-interactively.
commands.ping: Test network connectivity to a host.
commands.traceroute: Show the network path packets take to a destination.
commands.nslookup: Query DNS to look up IP addresses for domain names.
commands.dig: DNS lookup tool. More detail than nslookup.
commands.netstat: Display network connections, routing tables, and interface stats.
commands.ss: Display socket statistics (faster modern replacement for netstat).
commands.ifconfig: Configure or display network interface information (older).
commands.ip: Modern tool to show and manipulate network interfaces and routes.
commands.hostname: Show or set the system's hostname.
commands.uname: Print system information (kernel name, version, architecture).
commands.uptime: Show how long the system has been running.
commands.who: Show who is currently logged in.
commands.w: Show who is logged in and what they are doing.
commands.last: Show a list of recent logins.
commands.date: Print or set the system date and time.
commands.tar: Archive files into a single .tar file. Also used to extract archives.
commands.unzip: Extract files from a ZIP archive.
commands.gzip: Compress a file using gzip compression.
commands.bzip2: Compress a file using bzip2 compression (slower, smaller).
commands.xz: Compress a file using xz compression (very small output).
commands.7z: Archive and compress using 7-Zip format (must be installed).
commands.git: Version control system. Track changes in files over time.
commands.npm: Node Package Manager. Install and manage JavaScript packages.
commands.npx: Run a Node.js package without installing it globally.
commands.yarn: Alternative JavaScript package manager to npm.
commands.node: Run a JavaScript file with Node.js.
commands.python: Run a Python script or start the Python interactive shell.
commands.cargo: Rust package manager and build tool.
commands.go: Build, run, or test Go programs.
commands.make: Build automation tool. Runs instructions from a Makefile.
commands.clang: C/C++ compiler (used on macOS by default).
commands.brew: Homebrew package manager for macOS (and Linux).
commands.apt: Advanced Package Tool — install software on Debian/Ubuntu Linux.
commands.yum: Package manager for older Red Hat/CentOS Linux systems.
commands.dnf: Modern package manager for Fedora/RHEL Linux.
commands.open: Open a file with its default application (macOS).
commands.xdg-open: Open a file with its default application (Linux).
commands.pbcopy: Copy standard input to the clipboard (macOS).
commands.pbpaste: Paste clipboard contents to standard output (macOS).
commands.cron: Time-based job scheduler. Runs commands on a schedule.
commands.crontab: Edit the list of cron jobs for the current user.
commands.at: Schedule a one-time command to run later.
commands.watch: Run a command repeatedly and show output in real time.
commands.lsof: List open files and the processes using them.
commands.fuser: Show which processes are using a file or socket.
commands.strace: Trace system calls made by a process (Linux).
commands.ltrace: Trace library calls made by a process (Linux).
commands.dmesg: Print or control the kernel ring buffer (system messages).
commands.journalctl: Query the systemd journal (system log on modern Linux).
commands.logger: Write a message to the system log.
commands.systemctl: Control systemd services (start, stop, enable, status).
commands.service: Manage services on older Linux systems.
commands.screen: Terminal multiplexer. Run multiple sessions, detach and reattach.
commands.tmux: Modern terminal multiplexer. Split panes, multiple windows.
commands.vim: Powerful text editor. Press i to insert, Esc then :wq to save and quit.
commands.vi: Classic terminal text editor (predecessor to vim).
commands.emacs: Highly extensible text editor and development environment.
commands.diff3: Compare three files and show differences.
commands.cmp: Compare two files byte by byte.
commands.md5sum: Compute an MD5 checksum of a file.
commands.sha256sum: Compute a SHA-256 checksum of a file.
commands.openssl: Cryptography toolkit. Can generate keys, encrypt files, test TLS.
commands.gpg: GNU Privacy Guard. Encrypt, decrypt, and sign files.
commands.base64: Encode or decode data in Base64 format.
commands.xxd: Display a file in hexadecimal format.
commands.od: Dump file contents in various formats (octal, hex, etc.).
commands.strings: Extract printable strings from a binary file.
commands.nm: List symbols in an object file or library.
commands.ldd: List shared libraries required by a program.
commands.strip: Remove symbols from an object file.
commands.nmap: Network scanner. Discover hosts and open ports.
commands.ufw: Uncomplicated Firewall. Simpler front-end to iptables.
commands.firewall-cmd: Manage firewalld on Red Hat/Fedora systems.

flags.-a: All. Usually includes hidden files (starting with .) or all entries.
flags.-r: Recursive. Apply the command to all subdirectories as well.
flags.-f: Force. Override confirmation prompts. Also: follow (in tail -f).
flags.-v: Verbose. Show more detail about what the command is doing.
flags.-n: Number. Specify a count, or (in grep) show line numbers.
flags.-l: Long format (in ls). Also: list filenames only (in grep).
flags.-h: Human-readable. Show sizes in KB/MB/GB instead of raw bytes.
flags.-p: Parents. Create intermediate directories, or preserve permissions.
flags.-s: Silent, summary, or symbolic (depends on command).
flags.-d: Directory. Operate on directory itself, not its contents.
flags.-e: Expression. Specify a pattern or script on the command line.
flags.-x: Extended. Enable extended features or exclude a filesystem.
flags.-z: Compress/decompress with gzip, or suppress output if empty.
flags.--help: Show a brief usage summary for the command.
flags.--version: Show the version number of the command.
flags.--dry-run: Show what would happen without actually doing it.
